result:
The first time I realized that memory is not a reel but a loom, I was sitting on the back steps of a house I no longer own, watching a squirrel forget where it buried a walnut. The sun was low enough to stain the neighbor’s aluminum siding the color of dried blood, and the air carried the sweet, almost rotten scent of fallen pears that no one would ever harvest. I had lived in that house for eleven years, longer than I had lived anywhere, yet the steps felt provisional under my weight, as though the carpenter had only pretended to nail them down. Somewhere inside, my wife—ex-wife now—was packing the last of her books into liquor-store boxes, each carton labeled in her tidy architect’s script: KITCHEN / NON-FICTION / FRAGILE HOPE. I remember the phrase because I helped her write it, pressing the Sharpie into her palm the way you press a magnifying glass into sunlight, hoping for fire. Instead we got laughter, the bruised kind that recognizes the joke is on you.  

The squirrel darted back and forth across the yard, pausing to sniff the same patch of grass again and again, its small dark hand pressed to the earth like a dowser who no longer believes in water. I found myself praying, absurdly, that it would locate the nut, as if its success might reassure me that anything we bury can be found again: car keys, tempers, wedding rings, the name of the girl who sat behind me in seventh grade and once, without explanation, slipped a root-beer barrel into my shirt pocket. The squirrel leapt into the lilac, twigs shivering like gossip behind it. Nothing was recovered. The yard kept its secret.  

I tell you this because memory is not a reel but a loom, and every story I have ever told begins with threads that refuse to stay parallel. They cross, knot, double back, and sometimes, if you squint, form a pattern that looks like a plan. That evening on the steps I thought I was witnessing the disassembly of a life—my life—but in truth the disassembly had begun years earlier, probably on some ordinary Tuesday when I chose to answer an email instead of finishing a conversation, or when I laughed at a joke that wasn’t funny simply because I wanted to be liked. The walnut, the pear smell, the exile sunlight: these were not symptoms of collapse, only its witnesses.  

Years later, in a city on another coast, I found myself lecturing on extinct alphabets to a roomful of graduate students who were mainly interested in bulletproof syllabi. I had become—implausibly, accidentally—a professor of comparative paleography, a field so narrow it sometimes felt like a party trick. I taught them how to read the ghost-impressions of letters scraped off parchment, how to tilt a page under raking light until the erased words rose like bruises. One afternoon a student asked whether recovering lost texts was moral, since the scribes had erased them for a reason. I opened my mouth, then closed it, hearing the echo of my ex-wife’s laughter in the throat of a twenty-two-year-old who wore her anxiety like a prosthetic limb she was still learning to maneuver. The moment swelled until the entire seminar seemed to pulse with the unanswered question. Finally I said, “Every sentence is a survivor; our job is to listen to the scar.” The student nodded as if I had offered a key, though I had only handed her a mirror.  

That night I walked home along the river, which was really an estuary pretending to be civilized. The tide was out, exposing ribs of mud that stank of the nineteenth century—copper, coal tar, the metabolic sigh of whales rendered into lamp oil. I thought about the squirrel, about the walnut, about the invisible cartography of forgetting. I thought about how every marriage is an excavation site where future strangers will sift through our debris and mislabel the artifacts: this bowl was ceremonial, this receipt a love letter, this message in the drafts folder a prayer. My phone buzzed with a notification from a breathless app that promised to improve my “digital well-being” by reminding me I had stared at a screen for eleven hours. I thumbed it away the way you swat a moth that has mistaken your ear for a mate.  

At the river I stopped beneath a sodium lamp that turned the world the color of old Polaroids. Across the water, construction cranes hovered like constructive giraffes, their necks angled in polite curiosity. A sign advertised LUXURY AIR with prices per square foot that sounded like jokes without punch lines. I realized I had been reciting, soundlessly, the alphabet of a language no one had spoken for twelve hundred years. The letters tasted of iron and wet stone, the way a childhood blanket once tasted after it fell into the tub. I recited them backward, forward, scrambled into the prayers of a people who believed words could stitch a wound shut if you pronounced the consonants in the right order. The river kept breathing, indifferent as a god who has outsourced belief to algorithms.  

I tried, then, to remember the earliest thing. Not the story I had polished for therapists and first dates—“I was four, I lost my mother in a department store, I stood between racks of coats that smelled of mothballs and adult worry until her hand reached down like the dove returning to Noah”—but whatever preceded the polishing. I closed my eyes, felt the sodium light flutter against my lids, and dove. What surfaced was not a scene but a sensation: the nubby texture of a car-seat strap against my cheek, the metallic click of it being undone, the faint ammoniac puff of diaper cream. No images, only textures and temperatures. I could not tell if the strap was green or brown, if the car was parked or moving, if the adult leaning over me was mother, father, or stranger. Yet the memory felt truer than any photograph because it had no caption, no moral, no face. It was a shard of undigested time, glowing like a firefly inside the rib cage.  

The trouble with remembering is that it is indistinguishable from invention. Neuroscientists call it “reconsolidation”: each time you pull a memory out, you rewrite it slightly before putting it back. We are all unreliable narrators of our own lives, and yet we must stake marriages, mortgages, citizenship oaths on the parchment of our recollections. I once testified in court about a traffic accident I had seen the previous winter. While the lawyer jogged my memory with questions, the sedan in my mind changed color—from silver to charcoal to an iridescent blue that fluttered like a bruise—until I no longer knew which hue belonged to the actual car and which to the lawyer’s insinuations. When the judge asked whether I was certain, I said yes, because certainty is the currency we are asked to spend, even when our pockets are full of counterfeit.  

After the divorce I developed a habit of visiting secondhand bookstores and slipping photographs—ones I had never taken—between random pages. A sepia woman laughing on a pier, a child in a superhero cape glaring at a birthday cake, two men in 1940s bathing suits pretending to box. I bought the pictures in flea markets, bins labeled “Instant Ancestors.” My handwriting on the back always said the same thing: This was the day everything changed, but I signed a different name each time: your grandmother, the neighbor, the man who fixed the radio. I imagined future strangers discovering the images and feeling the vertigo of almost-recognition, the way you taste a spice you once ate in a country you can’t name. Perhaps they would invent stories to accommodate the ache. Perhaps they would show up at family gatherings brandishing proof of a relative no one else remembered. I was, I suppose, seeding the world with false memories so that my own fabrications might feel less lonely.  

One winter the city experienced a storm that meteorologists called a “bomb cyclone,” as though weather had graduated from metaphor to ordnance. The power went out for three days. I stayed in my apartment, wearing two coats indoors, reading a treatise on the transmission of Greek riddles through Arabic commentators by candle that smelled of failed apple pie. Outside, transformers sparked blue like tiny nebulae dying. On the second night I heard footsteps in the hallway—slow, deliberate, the gait of someone carrying fragile cargo. I opened the door to find an elderly woman clutching a shoebox. She wore a man’s trench coat over a nightgown printed with faded roses. “You teach letters,” she said, not a question. Before I could answer, she pressed the box into my hands. Inside lay a stack of celluloid reels no longer than index cards, the kind once used in handheld viewers. “They belong to someone who remembers,” she said, and turned away, her slippers whispering insults against the ice-cold tile.  

I waited until the next morning, when watery sunlight guilted its way through the blinds, to examine the reels. Each was labeled in fountain-pen ink: 1958 / Atlantic City / Sound of Rain. 1961 / Operating Theater / First Breath. 1964 / Unknown Living Room / Birthday Without Cake. I lacked the viewer, of course, but held the tiny strips to the window, watching inverted images flicker like the wings of pinned moths. In one, a toddler wobbled across parquet floor toward a cake shaped like a spaceship; in another, surgeons in fishbowl masks hovered over a sheeted form that might have been woman, man, or doll. The last reel was blank, or perhaps so overexposed that all detail had drowned in white. I strung them on a necklace of speculation: a life spliced into three-minute increments, bookmarked by occasions that refused to declare their significance.  

When power returned, I took the reels to the media lab and persuaded a technician to digitize them. We watched the files bloom on a monitor the size of a postcard. The sound had survived only as ghost-wave, a faint surf that might have been rain, applause, or applause impersonating rain. In the Atlantic City clip, a couple kissed beneath an awning while behind them a banner advertised the “Miss Atom Bomb” beauty pageant. The woman wore the same rose-print nightgown I had seen on the stranger in the hallway, though younger, defiantly alive. The man’s face was turned away, a blur of wanting. I felt the vertigo of recognition without evidence: Was this my parents? My ex-wife’s? A future I had misplaced? The technician, bored, scrolled ahead. In the operating-theater footage, the camera lingered on a wall clock whose second hand jerked in inconsistent increments, as though time itself had walking pneumonia. When the infant’s cry finally hacked through the static, I startled so violently I knocked over a can of seltzer. The lid rolled beneath the server rack, spinning like a coin whose bet no one remembered making.  

I never saw the woman again. I left notes on corkboards, asked the super, queried the mail carrier. The building swallowed her as neatly as a card trick. Sometimes I imagined she had been a manifestation of the city’s collective guilt over its forgotten films, its evaporated histories. More likely she was simply old, tired, and wanted to unburden herself of evidence before entering the next corridor. I kept the digitized files on an encrypted drive that I labeled “Taxes 1998” and tried not to open. Yet on nights when grading metastasized into existential audit, I clicked through the clips frame by frame, hunting for the moment the kiss transitioned from performance to confession, the instant the surgeon’s shoulders slumped in either grief or relief. I never found it. The pixels remained obediently ambiguous, the way a scar answers “I was there” but refuses to explain why.  

Teaching became a parallel archeology. I asked students to bring an object their families had misfiled—grandfather’s draft card, mother’s high-school debate trophy, a ticket stub for a concert no one remembered attending—and write the annotation that should have accompanied it. One woman produced a rusted dog tag inscribed with a name none of her living relatives recognized. Another offered a Polaroid of a cake shaped like the Kremlin, red icing slumping under the flash. We arrayed the artifacts on a seminar table like evidence at a trial whose crime kept shifting. I told them, “We are not repairing the past; we are loosening its stitches so it can breathe.” A young man in a knit cap that smelled of sheep and ambition asked whether loosening might also destroy. I said yes, but destruction is just renovation without a permit. He nodded as though I had gifted him a skeleton key, though I had only handed him a mirror.  

The semester tilted toward spring, which in that city arrived as rumor weeks before it materialized as weather. I began to dream in subtitles. My mother spoke in white Helvetica, my father in yellow Times New Roman; their captions sometimes overlapped, creating accidental haiku: “Don’t slam the / screen door / the past is allergic.” I woke laughing, a sound like water finding a crack in porcelain. On the first truly warm evening, I walked to the river carrying the shoebox reels. The tide was in, concealing the nineteenth-century ribs. I stood on the same spot where months earlier I had recited extinct alphabets, and instead spoke the only sentence that felt pressurized enough to contain all the others: “I release what I cannot verify.” Then I tipped the reels into the water one by one. They floated like dull mirrors before the current pulled them under. I felt neither catharsis nor regret, only the mild surprise of having carried something so far that, in the end, weighed so little.  

What followed was not an ending but an intermission. I finished the semester, declined the university’s offer of tenure, and moved into a studio above a bakery that specialized in funeral cookies—shortbread stamped with cherubs, lambs, hourglasses. At dawn the smell of butter and mourning drifted through the floorboards, and I woke believing I was inside a euphemism. I took a job transcribing oral histories for a museum that no longer possessed wall space to display them. All day I listened to refugees describe the taste of bread in cities that no longer existed, to retired longshoremen recount the weight of bananas before globalization, to retired dominatrixes parse the semiotics of safe words. Each interview ended with the same question: “What would you like the future to misremember about you?” Answers ranged from “my courage” to “my Social Security number.” One woman, a former lighthouse keeper, said simply, “The part where I was afraid.” I typed her sentence three times, as if repetition might grant it buoyancy.  

Some nights I walked the cemetery that bordered the bakery, reading names aloud to the dark, practicing the pronunciation of syllables that had died for want of a tongue. I imagined the reels somewhere downstream, nesting with shopping carts and Revolutionary War musket balls, the grain dissolving until only the concept of image remained. I thought about the squirrel, the walnut, the pear smell, the ex-wife’s handwriting on a box I maybe still owned, though I could no longer picture her face unless I saw it in a dream, where it appeared without warning, unlabeled, like footage from someone else’s vacation. I decided this was not failure but evolution: memory molting into myth, myth into metabolized calcium in the bones of strangers.  

The last time I saw the student who worried about morality, she was graduating, cap decorated with a QR code that linked to a manifesto about digital repatriation of looted selfies. She handed me a envelope containing a single developed photograph: the lighthouse keeper standing beside a lantern room, wind whipping her white hair into semaphore. On the back was a caption in the student’s spiky handwriting: She learned to navigate by what the dark omitted. I turned the image over and over, as though rotational inertia might decipher the sentence. Instead I felt the familiar vertigo, the taste of iron and wet stone. I asked the student whether she had taken the picture or found it. She smiled the way you smile when handed back a test you secretly hoped would remain ungraded, and said, “Does it matter? Someone was there.”  

I keep that photograph taped above my desk, next to a map of shorelines that have shifted since the map was printed. Some mornings I stand before both and recite the alphabet of the people who believed consonants could stitch wounds. The letters come out in different order each time, forming names I never utter aloud: the squirrel, the woman in the trench coat, the surgeon, the lighthouse keeper, the child who lost a mother between coat racks, the couple kissing under the banner that promised Miss Atom Bomb. They are not my memories, yet they weigh on me like inherited coins too worn to spend. I understand now that the loom never stops; every thread drags another behind it, visible only in the negative space where the fabric splits.  

Yesterday the bakery delivered a batch of experimental cookies stamped not with lambs or hourglasses but with open eyes. The owner, apologetic, said the mold had been mislabeled. I ate one anyway, tasting butter and surveillance. Then I walked to the river, which was neither river nor estuary but a moving archive of everything we have convinced ourselves to discard. I stood there reciting no alphabets, releasing no artifacts. The water kept breathing. The cookies dissolved into glucose and regret, the way all stories dissolve when digestion outruns testimony. I thought about certainty, about unreliability, about the sentence I once told a courtroom and the sentence I typed for a lighthouse keeper and the sentence you are reading now, which may outlive me in a server farm cooled by the evaporated breath of glaciers.  

If memory is a loom, then forgetting is the scissors—but a dull pair that leaves fuzzed edges, threads refusing to sever. We carry those unfinished hems like remoras, unaware they are feeding on our perpetual motion. I have stopped trying to locate the walnut. I have stopped auditioning faces for the man in the Atlantic City reel. I have stopped expecting the woman in the trench coat to knock. Instead, each morning I butter my toast with funeral spread, teach my tongue to recognize the taste of its own erasure, and practice signing the name that is mine today, knowing tomorrow it may belong to someone else who finds this page and thinks, “I was there.” The squirrel, I have decided, never needed the nut; it needed the burying, the ritual of preparing for a future it would probably misplace. I Needed the recitation, the release, the retrieval, the refusal. I needed you, reader, to inherit the alphabet and scatter it like seed that will not grow what you expect. Plant it anyway. Let the consonants rust into soil. Let the vowels rise as weeds that bloom into questions. Let the questions feed the moth who mistakes your ear for a mate. When it thrashes, listen. That flutter is the sound of time walking off its limp, the scar answering I was here, the fabric remembering the wound even after the body has moved on.

Someone was there.”  

I keep that photograph taped above my desk, next to a map of shorelines that have shifted since the map was printed. Some mornings I stand before both and recite the alphabet of the people who believed consonants could stitch wounds. The letters come out in different order each time, forming names I never utter aloud: the squirrel, the woman in the trench coat, the surgeon, the lighthouse keeper, the child who lost a mother between coat racks, the couple kissing under the banner that promised Miss Atom Bomb. They are not my memories, yet they weigh on me like inherited coins too worn to spend. I understand now that the loom never stops; every thread drags another behind it, visible only in the negative space where the fabric splits.  

Yesterday the bakery delivered a batch of experimental cookies stamped not with lambs or hourglasses but with open eyes. The owner, apologetic, said the mold had been mislabeled. I ate one anyway, tasting butter and surveillance. Then I walked to the river, which was neither river nor estuary but a moving archive of everything we have convinced ourselves to discard. I stood there reciting no alphabets, releasing no artifacts. The water kept breathing. The cookies dissolved into glucose and regret, the way all stories dissolve when digestion outruns testimony. I thought about certainty, about unreliability, about the sentence I once told a courtroom and the sentence I typed for a lighthouse keeper and the sentence you are reading now, which may outlive me in a server farm cooled by the evaporated breath of glaciers.  

If memory is a loom, then forgetting is the scissors—but a dull pair that leaves fuzzed edges, threads refusing to sever. We carry those unfinished hems like remoras, unaware they are feeding on our perpetual motion. I have stopped trying to locate the walnut. I have stopped auditioning faces for the man in the Atlantic City reel. I have stopped expecting the woman in the trench coat to knock. Instead, each morning I butter my toast with funeral spread, teach my tongue to recognize the taste of its own erasure, and practice signing the name that is mine today, knowing tomorrow it may belong to someone else who finds this page and thinks, “I was there.” The squirrel, I have decided, never needed the nut; it needed the burying, the ritual of preparing for a future it would probably misplace. I Needed the recitation, the release, the retrieval, the refusal. I needed you, reader, to inherit the alphabet and scatter it like seed that will not grow what you expect. Plant it anyway. Let the consonants rust into soil. Let the vowels rise as weeds that bloom into questions. Let the questions feed the moth who mistakes your ear for a mate. When it thrashes, listen. That flutter is the sound of time walking off its limp, the scar answering I was here, the fabric remembering the wound even after the body has moved on.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
